description: Cross-framework domain taxonomy for GCC regulatory harmonisation — v5.0 promotes Kuwait NBCC (NCSC Decision No. 2 of 2026 — National Basic Cybersecurity Controls) from pending to a first-class framework with 35 core controls across six NIST CSF functions plus a 16-control Cloud Security appendix. The NBCC is aligned with CIS Controls v8.1 IG1 and NIST CSF and references the National Data Classification Framework (Decision No. 1 of 2025). All other frameworks — including PDPL-KSA with its Implementing Regulation and Transfer Regulation — are unchanged from v4.0.

CLD-7-Shared responsibility documentation with entity accountability for Data Classification, IAM, and Resource Configuration
CLD-8-Real-time cloud asset inventory using programmatic resource tagging for classification and business owner
CLD-9-MFA for cloud management consoles and root accounts
CLD-10-Service account hygiene: no interactive human login use; periodic rotation or identity federation
CLD-11-Encryption by default for all cloud data at rest; CMEK for Sensitive data on risk basis
CLD-12-Customer Content residency per National Data Classification Framework
CLD-13-Operational Metadata exemption — metadata may process globally if kept non-sensitive
CLD-14-Public access prevention: storage resources block public access by default
CLD-15-Cloud audit logging (Admin Activity and Data Access) with 12-month retention
CLD-16-Secure connectivity with TLS 1.2+ and non-public management interfaces

domains.description: Industrial control system security, SCADA protection, OT network segmentation, and safety systems

QATAR-NIAS: NS5d-IPSEC/IPv6 for network security
NS5i-IPS/IDS to detect and prevent malicious activity
GS1-Networks protected from other networks by gateways
GS6-DMZs used to separate externally accessible systems

domains.description: PCI DSS controls, payment card data protection, transaction monitoring, and ATM/POS security
Change control processes, configuration baselines, release management, and hardening standards

PDPL-UAE: Art.8-2-Technical measures at design stage and during processing
Art.11-1-Reviewing privacy protection before new processing
PDPL-QAT: Art.11-1-Reviewing privacy protection before new processing operations
PDPL-KSA: Art.17-Amendments notified to all recipients of Personal Data
IR-Art.22.2.b-Correction notification to all prior recipients without delay
IR-Art.33.3-Controller ensures RoPA is accurate and up to date
QATAR-NIAS: CM1-Documented change management process Planned Major Maintenance Emergency categories
CM2-Cross-functional Change Management Committee with security and risk representation
CM3-All changes documented and approved through Change Management Committee
CM4-Security re-certification assessment on changes impacting ICT security
CM5-Associated documentation updated post-change
CM6-Emergency changes require verbal approval from Committee Head and post-emergency standard documentation
VL7-Change management process encompasses virtual technology environment
KUWAIT-NBCC: GOV-2-Documented exception process with approved risk acceptance and expiry date
PR-1-Configuration reviews after major changes; annual review baseline
GOV-1-Role and responsibility structure reviewed on major organisational changes

domains.description: Data subject rights (access, correction, erasure, portability, restriction), consent management, lawful basis for processing, and direct marketing controls

PDPL-QAT: Art.5-Right to withdraw consent and object
PDPL-KSA: Art.4-1-Right to be informed of legal basis and purpose
Art.4-2-Right to access Personal Data held by Controller
Art.4-3-Right to receive data in readable clear format (portability)
Art.4-4-Right to correction completion update
Art.4-5-Right to request Destruction when no longer needed
Art.5-Consent required for Processing and purpose change
Art.5-2-Right to withdraw consent at any time
Art.6-Processing without consent (vital interests, law, public entity security, legitimate interest)
Art.7-Consent cannot be conditioned on unrelated service provision
Art.9-Controller time frames and limits on access right
Art.13-Information at Collection (legal basis, purpose, recipients, cross-border, consequences, rights)
Art.21-Response to Data Subject requests within Regulations-specified period
Art.25-Marketing/awareness materials opt-in and opt-out mechanism
Art.26-Marketing consent for non-sensitive data collected directly
Art.27-Research/statistical exemption if anonymised or identity destroyed before disclosure
Art.28-Restriction on copying official identifiable documents
IR-Art.3-Rights requests acted on within 30 days (extendable 30) with identity verification and documentation
IR-Art.4-Right to be informed at direct collection (seven elements) and within 30 days at indirect collection
IR-Art.5-Right of access via channel or request without prejudicing others
IR-Art.6-Right to request a copy in commonly-used electronic format
IR-Art.7-Right to restrict processing when accuracy is contested; Controller may request evidence
IR-Art.8-Right to request destruction including backups and notification to downstream recipients
IR-Art.10-Data Subject choice of communication means (email, SMS, national address, apps)
IR-Art.11-Consent form, conditions, independent per purpose; explicit for Sensitive, Credit, automated decisions
IR-Art.12-Consent withdrawal procedures, cessation, and downstream notification
IR-Art.13-Legal guardian exercises rights and gives consent for those lacking full legal capacity
IR-Art.30-Scientific, research, statistical exemption: pseudonymisation where possible, minimisation, no adverse impact
IR-Art.37-Complaint to SDAIA within 90 days with specified content

domains.description: Secure information sharing between organisations, gateway controls, data import/export filtering, and cross-domain connectivity

PDPL-KSA: Art.15-Disclosure rules and conditions
Art.16-Disclosure restrictions (9 categories)
Art.17-Amendment notification to transferees
Art.29-Cross-border Transfer conditions and adequacy
IR-Art.15-Third-party collection safeguards: necessity, proportionality, Anonymisation
IR-Art.20-Disclosure safeguards: purpose proximity, privacy preservation, minimisation, pseudonymisation of third-party identities, RoPA logging
IR-Art.21-Public Entity disclosure for public interest: necessity, mandate proximity, damage limitation, RoPA logging
TR-Art.2-Cross-border Disclosure general conditions
TR-Art.6-Exemption-based cross-border Disclosure
QATAR-NIAS: IE1-Cross-domain risk evaluation prior to connectivity
IE2-Network-to-network connection risk review for cascaded connections
IE3-Confidentiality agreements prior to information exchange NDA
IE4-Exchange media protected from unauthorised access
IE5-Maintain classification of information received from other organisations
IE7-Trusted courier services only
IE8-Electronic messaging protected from unauthorised access
IE9-S/MIME or equivalent for C3+ information
IE12-Authenticated encrypted channels for C2/I2+ transmissions
IE13-Public information limited to sanitised approved content via designated spokesperson
GS1-Networks protected from other networks by gateways
GS4-Dual control four-eyes for C3+ gateway management
GS6-DMZs separate external from internal
GS7-Gateways deny all by default allow only explicitly authorised
GS8-Gateways hardened against malicious code DoS escalation
GS9-Gateway monitoring with threat prevention logging alerts
GS10-Content filtering offensive material malware DoS
GS13-Export filtering by classification label
GS14-Keyword search on textual data exports
GS16-Data import approval process
GS17-Imported data scanned for malicious content
KUWAIT-NBCC: PR-3.1-Official social media and digital presence: central register of entity accounts on external platforms with corporate email addresses and MFA
PR-4.1-Entity-approved communication and collaboration platforms only; private messaging, personal email, unauthorised remote-control tools prohibited for work
GOV-6-Service provider incident reporting mechanisms and timeframes defined

CBK.name: CBK Cybersecurity Framework for Kuwaiti Banking Sector

PDPL-UAE.name: UAE Federal Decree-Law No. 45 of 2021 on Personal Data Protection
sector: All sectors handling personal data of UAE residents
PDPL-UAE.enforcer: UAE Data Bureau (established under Federal Decree-Law No. 44 of 2021)
PDPL-UAE.notes: Excludes government entities, security/judicial authorities, health data with separate legislation, banking/credit data with separate legislation, and free zone companies with their own data protection regimes (e.g. DIFC, ADGM)

PDPL-QAT.name: Qatar Law No. 13 of 2016 on Protecting Personal Data Privacy
sector: All sectors handling personal data electronically
PDPL-QAT.enforcer: Ministry of Transport and Communications (Competent Department)
PDPL-QAT.notes: Applies to electronically processed personal data. Excludes personal/family use and official statistical data. Penalties up to 5M QAR for breaches of security controls.

PDPL-KSA.name: Saudi Arabia Personal Data Protection Law
PDPL-KSA.version: Royal Decree M/19 of 2021, amended by M/148 of 2023, as supplemented by the SDAIA Implementing Regulation and the Regulation on Personal Data Transfer outside the Kingdom (both issued September 2023)
sector: All sectors processing personal data of individuals residing in the Kingdom
PDPL-KSA.enforcer: Saudi Data and AI Authority (SDAIA) — Competent Authority under the Law
PDPL-KSA.legalBasis: Royal Decree No. M/19 dated 09/02/1443 AH (16/09/2021) — amended by Royal Decree No. M/148 dated 05/09/1444 AH (27/03/2023)
PDPL-KSA.effectiveDate: 14 September 2023 (with one-year grace period ending September 2024)

implementingRegulations.summary: PDPL-KSA is a single regulatory framework comprising THREE instruments: (a) the Personal Data Protection Law (Royal Decree M/19 of 2021, amended by M/148 of 2023), (b) the Implementing Regulation of the Personal Data Protection Law, issued by SDAIA in September 2023, and (c) the Regulation on Personal Data Transfer outside the Kingdom, issued by SDAIA in September 2023. Control references use the prefix Art.X for Law articles, IR-Art.X for Implementing Regulation articles, and TR-Art.X for Transfer Regulation articles.

instruments.name: Personal Data Protection Law
instruments.issuer: Council of Ministers via Royal Decree
instruments.reference: Royal Decree M/19 of 09/02/1443 AH, amended by Royal Decree M/148 of 05/09/1444 AH

instruments.name: Implementing Regulation of the Personal Data Protection Law
instruments.reference: Issued September 2023 alongside Law enforcement; 38 articles
instruments.scopeNote: Operationalises the Law: specifies timelines (data subject response 30 days, breach notification 72 hours, RoPA retention +5 years, complaint window 90 days), consent forms, DPO triggers, DPIA scope, and legitimate-interest assessment.

instruments.name: Regulation on Personal Data Transfer outside the Kingdom
instruments.reference: Issued September 2023 alongside Law enforcement; 10 articles across 4 chapters
instruments.scopeNote: Regulates cross-border transfer through adequacy decisions issued by the Prime Minister on SDAIA recommendation, appropriate safeguards (Binding Common Rules, Standard Contractual Clauses, certifications, binding codes of conduct), and limited exemption cases. Mandates a written risk assessment for transfers under safeguards, exemption-based transfers, and continuous/large-scale transfers of Sensitive Data. Adequacy decisions are reviewed every 4 years.

PDPL-KSA.extraterritorial: Yes — Article 2 applies the Law to any processing of personal data of individuals residing in the Kingdom, including processing from outside the Kingdom

penalties.sensitiveDataDisclosure: Up to 2 years imprisonment or fine up to SAR 3M or both (Art.35) — doubled for recidivism
penalties.otherViolations: Warning or fine up to SAR 5M (Art.36) — doubled for repeat violations
penalties.civilCompensation: Data Subject may claim proportionate compensation for material or moral damage (Art.40)

PDPL-KSA.notes: Covers deceased persons' data where identifying them or a family member (Art.2). Exempts personal/family use only (Art.2; IR-Art.2). Sensitive Data includes racial/ethnic origin, religious/intellectual/political belief, criminal convictions, biometric/genetic data, health data, and data indicating unknown parentage. Cross-border transfer regime is now fully operational via the SDAIA Transfer Regulation (TR-Art.2-8). Banking/credit data regulated jointly with SAMA and under the Credit Information Law (Art.24, Art.30-1; IR-Art.27). Health data has additional restrictions (Art.23; IR-Art.26). Information security controls explicitly defer to NCA controls where applicable (IR-Art.23.2) — implementing NCA-ECC can satisfy PDPL-KSA Art.23 / IR-Art.23 information-security obligations. Must coordinate with NCA on cybersecurity aspects per Art.42.

breachNotificationToAuthority.window: 72 hours from the Controller becoming aware of the incident
breachNotificationToAuthority.staggeredReporting: Permitted under IR-Art.24.2 with written justification for the delay
breachNotificationToAuthority.dataSubjectNotification: Without undue delay, if breach may cause damage or conflict with rights (IR-Art.24.5)

dataSubjectRightsResponse.window: 30 days from receipt of the request
dataSubjectRightsResponse.extension: Up to an additional 30 days if disproportionate effort or multiple requests; Data Subject must be notified in advance with reasons

indirectCollectionInformationDuty.window: 30 days from the point of collection (without undue delay)

complaintFilingWindow.window: 90 days from the incident or the date the Data Subject became aware
complaintFilingWindow.extension: Competent Authority discretion to accept late complaints for reasonable cause

recordsOfProcessingRetention.window: For the duration of processing plus 5 years from the date processing ends

adequacyReviewCycle.window: Every 4 years, or sooner if necessary
adequacyReviewCycle.reviewable: Prime Minister may terminate, amend, or suspend adequacy decisions on SDAIA recommendation (TR-Art.4.4)

requiredWhen: Controller is a Public Entity providing services that involve Processing Personal Data on a large scale
Controller's primary activities consist of Processing operations requiring regular and continuous monitoring of individuals on a large scale
Controller's core activities consist of Processing sensitive Personal Data
dpoTriggers.status: May be an official, employee, or external contractor (IR-Art.32.2). SDAIA will issue further appointment rules (IR-Art.32.4).
dpoTriggers.largeScaleDefinition: Not numerically defined in the Law, Implementing Regulation, or Transfer Regulation. SDAIA may issue guidance; do NOT invent a numeric threshold when reasoning.

required: Collecting, comparing, or linking two or more Personal Data sets from different sources
Continuous and large-scale Processing of Personal Data of individuals lacking full legal capacity
Processing operations requiring continuous monitoring of Data Subjects
Processing Personal Data using new technologies
Decisions made based on automated Processing of Personal Data
Providing a product or service involving Processing likely to cause serious harm to Data Subject privacy
dpiaTriggers.elements: Purpose and legal basis; nature, types and sources of data; scope including geography; context including relationships; necessity and proportionality; impact severity and likelihood; mitigating measures; suitability of mitigating measures (IR-Art.25.2)
dpiaTriggers.distinctFromLIA: IR-Art.25 DPIA (impact assessment) and IR-Art.16.3 Legitimate Interest Assessment are DISTINCT instruments. An LIA is required before processing on legitimate-interest basis; a DPIA is required in the seven scenarios above regardless of legal basis. A single processing activity may require BOTH.

layeredRegime: Step 1 — Adequacy: Prime Minister issues an adequacy decision on SDAIA recommendation based on TR-Art.3.1 criteria (laws, rule of law, enforcement effectiveness, Data Subject remedies, supervisory authority, cooperation, disclosure rules)

Step 2 — Appropriate safeguards (where adequacy absent): Binding Common Rules (TR-Art.5.1.a, TR-Art.5.2); SDAIA-approved Standard Contractual Clauses (TR-Art.5.1.b); certifications from accredited entities (TR-Art.5.1.c); Binding Codes of Conduct approved by SDAIA (TR-Art.5.1.d)

Step 3 — Exemption-based transfers (where safeguards unavailable): performance of agreement with Data Subject; public-entity national security or public interest; public-entity crime investigation/prosecution; protection of Vital Interests of unreachable Data Subject (TR-Art.6)

Step 4 — Risk assessment: mandatory written assessment before any transfer under safeguards, exemptions, or continuous/large-scale Sensitive Data transfers (TR-Art.8.1), covering purpose, nature, safeguards, minimisation, impact, and mitigation (TR-Art.8.2)
crossBorderTransfer.stopConditions: Controller must immediately stop cross-border transfer if it affects national security or Vital Interests of the Kingdom, if risk assessment reveals high privacy risk, or if appropriate safeguards become unenforceable (TR-Art.7)

referencedButUnmappedAuthorities: National Cybersecurity Authority (NCA) — IR-Art.23.2 defers information-security controls to NCA controls where applicable, creating an explicit bridge to NCA-ECC

Digital Government Authority (DGA) — IR-Art.35 and IR-Art.36 require SDAIA coordination with DGA for accreditation and auditing entities serving government entities; DGA framework not currently in taxonomy
Saudi Central Bank (SAMA) — IR-Art.27 requires Credit Data processors to adopt SAMA rules on top of PDPL
Ministry of Health, Saudi Health Council, Council of Health Insurance — IR-Art.26 requires Health Data processors to adopt their rules on top of PDPL

QATAR-NIAS.name: Qatar National Information Assurance Standard
sector: All organisations operating within the State of Qatar
QATAR-NIAS.legalBasis: Amiri Decree No. 1 of 2021 establishing NCSA
QATAR-NIAS.certificationBody: NCSA Cyber Assurance Department — accepts NIA V2.1 certification applications from May 2023
QATAR-NIAS.notes: Applies to all Qatar organisations and their information assets including outsourced and subcontracted activities. Baseline controls (marked *) are mandatory for assets rated I1/A1/C1 or above. Additional controls required for I2/A2/C2 (one or more) and I3/A3/C3 (two or more) based on risk assessment. Must be used in conjunction with National Data Classification Policy [IAP-NAT-DCLS]. NIAP V2.0 certificates remain valid; re-certification to V2.1 required for recertification. V2.0 applications accepted until 31 Dec 2023.

KUWAIT-NBCC.name: Kuwait National Basic Cybersecurity Controls
KUWAIT-NBCC.version: NCSC Decision No. 2 of 2026, Annex (1) — English Annex is the authoritative technical text
sector: All civil government, military, security agencies, and private sector entities within Kuwait that fall under the NCSC mandate as defined in Amiri Decree 37 of 2022. Other public and private entities not formally within this mandate are strongly encouraged to adopt the baseline voluntarily.
KUWAIT-NBCC.enforcer: National Cyber Security Center (NCSC) — competent national authority established by Amiri Decree 37 of 2022
KUWAIT-NBCC.legalBasis: Amiri Decree 37 of 2022 establishing NCSC; Cabinet Decision 117 of 2022 (supervising minister); Cabinet Decision 166 of 2025 (appointing NCSC Chairman); Decision 35 of 2023 (National Cybersecurity Governance Framework); Decision 1 of 2025 (National Data Classification Framework)
KUWAIT-NBCC.effectiveDate: Published in Kuwait Al-Youm (Official Gazette) Issue 1785 on 5 April 2026; decision signed 31 March 2026
KUWAIT-NBCC.complianceDeadline: 18 months from Official Gazette publication date (Article 7) — target full compliance by early October 2027. Exceptions require documented, time-bound NCSC approval with justified risk and compensating controls where applicable.
KUWAIT-NBCC.structure: NIST CSF 2.0 aligned — six functions: GOV (Govern), ID (Identify), PR (Protect), DE (Detect), RS (Respond), RC (Recover). 35 core controls total. Each control carries a Minimum Requirement expressed in MUST/SHOULD/MAY terms. A mandatory Cloud Security Appendix (Appendix A) adds 16 controls (CLD-1 through CLD-16) covering cloud acquisition, governance, IAM, data protection/sovereignty, and operational security. Aligned with CIS Controls v8.1 Implementation Group 1 (IG1) and NIST CSF.
KUWAIT-NBCC.languageAuthority: Article 6 specifies that the Annex remains in English and serves as the authoritative technical text for the National Basic Cybersecurity Controls. Arabic decision text governs the legal framing; English Annex governs the technical requirements.
kuwaitSpecificDistinctives: GOV-3 data sovereignty: storing or processing Sensitive data outside Kuwait requires explicit NCSC approval under Decision 1 of 2025 before use
GOV-4 Kuwaitization: prioritise qualified Kuwaiti nationals for sensitive cyber roles (SOC analysts, administrators, incident responders) consistent with national HR frameworks
PR-2.1 corporate email only: personal/consumer email accounts MUST NOT be configured on corporate devices and MUST NOT be used for work communication
PR-4.1 approved communications: entity-approved platforms only for official work; private messaging, personal email, unauthorised remote-control tools prohibited
CLD-1 CSP regulatory authorisation: Cloud Service Providers must be authorised to operate in Kuwait in accordance with national authority regulations
CLD-12 data residency: Customer Content residency rules apply per National Data Classification Framework (Decision 1 of 2025)
RS-1 incident reporting timelines are set by NCSC (not numerically fixed in the baseline itself; refer to separately issued NCSC incident-management guidance)
KUWAIT-NBCC.notes: The NBCC is explicitly a MINIMUM BASELINE and starting point, not an end state. Entities MAY implement stronger or additional controls based on risk profile, legal obligations, or sector-specific requirements. Where sector regulators impose stricter requirements (e.g. CBK for banking), Article 4 of Decision 2/2026 requires compliance with the stricter standard without prejudice to NBCC and NCSC authority. Undefined technical terms default to the NIST glossary unless otherwise defined in national instruments. Annual self-assessments using NCSC-issued checklists are mandatory (GOV-5) with records retained for at least 3 years.

pdplTriggers.description: PDPL frameworks are triggered by data handling, not just geography. Include PDPL-UAE when processing personal data of UAE residents. Include PDPL-QAT when processing personal data of Qatar residents. Include PDPL-KSA when processing personal data of individuals residing in Saudi Arabia (Art.2 extraterritorial). All three PDPLs can apply simultaneously to an organisation processing GCC-wide personal data. PDPL-KSA additionally requires compliance with the Implementing Regulation (IR-Art.X) and, for any data leaving the Kingdom, the Regulation on Personal Data Transfer outside the Kingdom (TR-Art.X).

PDPL-UAE.trigger: Processing personal data of any natural person who is a UAE resident, or operating as a Controller/Processor located in UAE
exemptions: Personal health data with separate legislation
Banking and credit data with separate legislation
DIFC and ADGM free zone companies with own data protection regimes

PDPL-QAT.trigger: Electronically processing personal data of Qatar residents

PDPL-KSA.trigger: Processing personal data of any individual residing in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, by any means, including processing by parties outside the Kingdom (extraterritorial per Art.2). Also applies to data of deceased persons where it could identify them or a family member.
exemptions: Personal or family use not disclosed outside the family or limited social circle (IR-Art.2); purely professional/commercial/non-profit uses are never personal-or-family use
Banking and credit data regulated jointly with Saudi Central Bank (SAMA) under separate frameworks (Art.30-1; IR-Art.27)
PDPL-KSA.sensitiveDataDefinition: Racial or ethnic origin; religious, intellectual or political belief; criminal convictions and offenses; biometric or genetic data for identification; health data; data indicating unknown parentage (Art.1-11)
PDPL-KSA.implementingRegulationTrigger: Whenever PDPL-KSA applies, the Implementing Regulation applies by default — there is no separate applicability test. Treat them as one framework for selection purposes.
PDPL-KSA.transferRegulationTrigger: Applies whenever the Controller transfers Personal Data outside the Kingdom or discloses it to a party outside the Kingdom, regardless of the transfer mechanism. A mandatory written risk assessment is required for transfers relying on appropriate safeguards (TR-Art.5), exemption cases (TR-Art.6), or involving continuous or large-scale Sensitive Data (TR-Art.8). Include whenever cloud processors, SaaS vendors, group companies, or subcontractors are outside the Kingdom.

SDAIA-DPO-RULES.name: SDAIA Rules for Data Protection Officer Appointment
SDAIA-DPO-RULES.legalBasis: IR-Art.32.4 — SDAIA shall issue rules for DPO appointment
SDAIA-DPO-RULES.status: pending — referenced by the Implementing Regulation but not yet issued at the time of v4.0 taxonomy authorship. Upload via custom framework feature when available.
SDAIA-DPO-RULES.impact: Will clarify "large scale" threshold and specific DPO appointment scenarios currently left open in IR-Art.32.1

SDAIA-NATIONAL-REGISTER.name: SDAIA National Register of Controllers — Registration Rules
SDAIA-NATIONAL-REGISTER.legalBasis: IR-Art.34 — SDAIA shall issue the rules for registration including which Controllers are required to register
SDAIA-NATIONAL-REGISTER.status: pending — referenced by the Implementing Regulation. When issued, will add an explicit registration-duty control to policy-compliance domain.

SDAIA-ACCREDITATION-AUDIT-RULES.name: SDAIA Accreditation and Audit Body Licensing Rules
SDAIA-ACCREDITATION-AUDIT-RULES.issuedBy: Saudi Data and AI Authority (SDAIA), coordinated with Digital Government Authority for government services
SDAIA-ACCREDITATION-AUDIT-RULES.legalBasis: IR-Art.35 (accreditation), IR-Art.36 (audit) — SDAIA shall issue regulatory rules for licensing entities
SDAIA-ACCREDITATION-AUDIT-RULES.status: pending — referenced by the Implementing Regulation. When issued, will populate the policy-compliance domain with specific audit/accreditation control obligations.

SDAIA-SCC.name: SDAIA Standard Contractual Clauses and Binding Common Rules Templates
SDAIA-SCC.legalBasis: TR-Art.5.1.b (SCC standard model), TR-Art.5.1.a (BCR approval), TR-Art.9 (guidelines)
SDAIA-SCC.status: pending — SDAIA has stated it will issue the standard contractual clauses model and guidelines. Until issued, cross-border transfers lacking an adequacy decision must rely on bespoke safeguards with SDAIA case-by-case approval.

SDAIA-ADEQUACY-LIST.name: SDAIA Adequacy Decisions — List of Adequate Countries, Sectors, and International Organisations
SDAIA-ADEQUACY-LIST.status: pending at v4.0 authorship — no public adequacy decisions have been issued. Cross-border transfers must currently rely on appropriate safeguards (TR-Art.5) or exemption cases (TR-Art.6) supported by risk assessment (TR-Art.8).
